comment_text: Boundary rulings should prioritize clear line contact and reliable camera perspective; avoid overconfident edge calls from distorted angles.
When evidence is mixed, state what is known, what is uncertain, and keep the on-court call unless replay standard is met.
Use on-court lineup context to resolve likely player identity when jersey view is limited.
For contact decisions, anchor language to legal guarding position, control, and shooting motion definitions before declaring foul type.
Reference period and game clock context when timing influences penalty administration or continuation.
Identify whether contact occurred on a live ball versus dead-ball interval; this can change whether play is reviewable or penalized as away-from-play.
When whistle timing and shot clock reset are disputed, separate foul determination from reset determination and state both clearly.
Last-touch rulings should focus on the final visible contact point immediately before the ball becomes out of bounds.
After confirming foul type, verify whether penalty state leads to free throws, side-out, or no shots.
Do not conflate violation evidence with contact evidence; determine violation status independently from foul contact status.
If basket interference/goaltending is not implicated, avoid introducing Rule 11 rationale into unrelated contact calls.
examples: drive contact with no rim touch

comment_text: For shooting and away-from-play fouls, describe who initiated illegal contact, where it occurred, and whether it affected rhythm/speed/balance/freedom of movement.
Only overturn when video is clear and conclusive; otherwise confirm the floor call.
When challenge context is present, mention challenge relevance only as procedure context; the ruling itself must still come from evidence and rule criteria.
